text: Welcome to this video from James Cook University Hospital. My name is Joel Dunning and I'd like to present to you a right-sided thymectomy. This is the CT scan where you can see the thymic mass which goes just over the innominate vein into the neck. It's a fairly decent sized one and there were some concerns over adhesions.

The surgeon inserts the second port into the chest through the second intercostal space at the midclavicular line, following the first port placed through the fifth intercostal space at the anterior axillary line. A third port is introduced, and the camera view is adjusted to a higher perspective. The pericardium, diaphragm, and visible adhesions are observed.

The surgeon uses a hook ligature device to carefully dissect the right lung from the thymus. The phrenic nerve is visible adjacent to the superior vena cava (SVC), and the ligasure technology is employed to seal small vessels like the thymic vein with minimal heat dissipation, ensuring no damage to the phrenic nerve.

The surgeon clears fatty tissue from the diaphragmatic recess using a hook, ensuring thorough removal of thymus and fatty tissue. A sub-xiphoid port is utilized, accommodating a 12mm trocar with a 30-degree camera for viewing the opposite phrenic nerve. The surgeon opens the left pleura beyond the diaphragm, using CO₂ insufflation at 8 to 10 mmHg to enhance visibility.

The surgeon enters the left pleura and restores ventilation to both lungs using a double lumen tube. CO₂ insufflation is applied to depress both lungs throughout the procedure. The surgeon proceeds to excise a large disc of pericardium along with the thymic mass, ensuring complete resection and minimizing the risk of incomplete pathology assessment.

The surgeon strips the thymus and thymic fat from below upwards, carefully navigating around the pericardiofrenic vessels using a ligature device. The dissection follows the thymus border laterally towards the phrenic nerve, ensuring no thymic tissue is left behind. The pleural reflection is opened beyond the rhema vein, revealing the superior vena cava (SVC) and the innominate vein tracking medially.

The surgeon uses a hook to dissect medial to the rhema vein over the thymic mass, addressing small vessels with a ligasure. The thymus is stripped off the back of the sternum, and the opposite pleura is opened widely. The pericardium is opened, revealing a clear view with no adhesions and no invasion of the thymic mass through the pericardium.

The surgeon opens the pericardium widely, ensuring no further invasion of the thymic mass. The reamer vein is clearly visible as the surgeon carefully dissects the fatty tissue and thymus from it, anticipating vessels from the reamer and lemur veins and arteries.

The surgeon dissects over the innominate vein, anticipating the presence of a thymic vein extending into the thymus. Using a ligature, the surgeon cauterizes vessels encountered during dissection. The thymic mass is notably large and adherent, prompting the surgeon to focus on easier sections first. A 12mm vertical incision is made below the xiphi sternum for a sub-xiphoid port, allowing insertion above the diaphragm. The camera is used to visualize the left phrenic nerve.

The surgeon uses a sub-xiphoid camera and instruments from the right side to manipulate the thymoma, encountering adhesions to the lungs on both sides. The dissection continues over the innominate vein, searching for the lemur and reamer veins and arteries. A sizable vessel is identified, suitable for energy application. The surgeon tracks up into the right superior horn, aiming to remove all tissue up to the thyro-thymic ligament, noting the presence of tumor extending further superiorly than anticipated.

The surgeon uses energy to divide the Rheema vein, improving the view for dissecting the right superior horn of the thymus up to the thyrothyromic ligament. The innominate vein is visible tracking underneath, and a vein from the SVC is managed with ligature. The innominate artery is exposed, confirming the correct dissection plane. The surgeon pulls down the right superior horn, ensuring no fatty tissue remains above, and prepares to cut across the tissue.

The surgeon addresses the vessels of the thyrothymic ligament using energy devices, ensuring a dry surgical field with the ligature. Three 5mm ports are utilized in the right chest, with instruments including a 5mm peanut and a trumpet sucker to manage bleeding and maintain CO₂ insufflation.

The surgeon elevates the patient's head to reduce venous pressure and uses a Johan blunt tip dissector and ligature to dissect across the innominate vein, identifying and controlling the thymic vein with energy. The left superior horn is pulled downwards using the ligature, with the lima vein visible in the distance. The surgeon achieves a clear view of the left horn from the right-hand side, retracting it into the opposite chest for better access. The lima vein and artery are ligated to manage the blood supply.

The surgeon uses a sub-xiphoid camera and a hook to create a disc of pericardium. Due to lung adherence to the thymus, a 12-mm tri-stapler is used to detach the lung from the thymic mass. The thymus mass is pulled into the right chest to improve the view of the opposite phrenic nerve. A new 5-mm port is placed in the left chest, providing a clearer view of the phrenic nerve, allowing the surgeon to use a ligature to continue dissection along the pericardium, getting close to the phrenic nerve.

The surgeon uses a single five-millimeter port on the left side to maintain vision of the phrenic nerve while lifting the tumor. The ligasure device is used to carefully divide the tumor from the phrenic nerve with minimal heat dissipation. The divided piece of lung is visible, and the pericardium is safely divided once the phrenic nerve is pushed away. The pulmonary artery and innominate vein are visible, and the tumor acts as a retractor to pull away from the pulmonary artery.

The surgeon completes the final anatomical dissection with the phrenic nerve visible to the right of the ligature. The tumor is pulled back into the right chest, with both lungs fully inflated and CO₂ pressure maintained at ten millimeters of mercury. The innominate vein is visible in the distance, and the surgeon uses a hook to pull the tumor towards themselves, removing the remaining fatty tissue underneath the innominate vein and above the pulmonary artery, while addressing the last bit of the pericardial reflection.

The surgeon uses a hook ligature for dissection, functioning as scissors, a grasper, and a hook. Three instruments are visible in the chest for the final part of the procedure. A bag is inserted through the sub-xiphoid port to remove the thymic tumor, which is marked for pathology. The tumor is placed aside while checking for bleeding. A size twenty French drain is inserted post-operatively due to lung stapling.

Thank you very much for watching. Here's a quick video about the hook ligature which is great showing it's multifaceted usage. It's very easy to use for sealing, for grasping, cutting and of course changing over to becoming a hook. Thank you very much for watching. I hope you've enjoyed it and please do let me know what you think. This is from myself, Joel Dunning. Thanks for watching.
